name: deployments
context: Create, scale, update, and rollback Kubernetes Deployments — the standard way to run stateless applications
info: Deployments are the primary workload controller in Kubernetes for stateless applications. A Deployment declares a desired state (image, replicas, update strategy) and the Deployment controller ensures the cluster matches it by managing ReplicaSets. Agents use Deployments to deploy applications, scale replicas up/down, perform rolling updates with zero downtime, and rollback to previous versions when releases fail. This skill covers Deployment manifests, rollout strategies (RollingUpdate, Recreate), scaling, rollback, and the relationship between Deployments, ReplicaSets, and Pods.
body:
# Deployments

A Deployment provides declarative updates for [[kubernetes/pods]]. You describe a desired state, and the Deployment controller changes the actual state to match. Deployments manage ReplicaSets, which in turn manage Pods.

## Deployment Manifest

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: web-app
  namespace: production
  labels:
    app: web-app
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: web-app
  strategy:
    type: RollingUpdate
    rollingUpdate:
      maxSurge: 1
      maxUnavailable: 0
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web-app
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: web
          image: myregistry/web-app:v1.2.0
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8080
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: 200m
              memory: 256Mi
            limits:
              cpu: 1000m
              memory: 1Gi
          livenessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /healthz
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 10
            periodSeconds: 15
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /ready
              port: 8080
            initialDelaySeconds: 5
            periodSeconds: 10
          envFrom:
            - configMapRef:
                name: web-app-config
            - secretRef:
                name: web-app-secrets
```

## kubectl Deployment Commands

```bash
# Create / update deployment
kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml

# Quick create without a manifest
kubectl create deployment web-app --image=myregistry/web-app:v1.2.0 --replicas=3

# List deployments
kubectl get deployments -n production
kubectl get deploy -o wide

# Describe
kubectl describe deployment web-app

# Scale replicas
kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=5

# Update image (triggers rolling update)
kubectl set image deployment/web-app web=myregistry/web-app:v1.3.0

# Check rollout status
kubectl rollout status deployment/web-app

# View rollout history
kubectl rollout history deployment/web-app

# Rollback to previous revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app

# Rollback to specific revision
kubectl rollout undo deployment/web-app --to-revision=3

# Pause and resume rollout (for canary-style partial rollouts)
kubectl rollout pause deployment/web-app
kubectl rollout resume deployment/web-app

# Restart all pods (rolling restart)
kubectl rollout restart deployment/web-app
```

## Update Strategies

### RollingUpdate (default)

Gradually replaces old Pods with new ones:

```yaml
strategy:
  type: RollingUpdate
  rollingUpdate:
    maxSurge: 1        # max pods above desired count during update
    maxUnavailable: 0  # max pods unavailable during update (0 = zero downtime)
```

- `maxSurge: 1, maxUnavailable: 0` — safest: create new Pod, wait for ready, then terminate old
- `maxSurge: 25%, maxUnavailable: 25%` — faster: allows 25% over and 25% under simultaneously

### Recreate

Kills all existing Pods before creating new ones. Use when the app cannot run two versions simultaneously (e.g., database schema conflicts):

```yaml
strategy:
  type: Recreate
```

## Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod Hierarchy

```
Deployment (web-app)
  └── ReplicaSet (web-app-6d9f8b7c5d)  ← current
       ├── Pod (web-app-6d9f8b7c5d-abc12)
       ├── Pod (web-app-6d9f8b7c5d-def34)
       └── Pod (web-app-6d9f8b7c5d-ghi56)
  └── ReplicaSet (web-app-4a8e2c1b3f)  ← previous (scaled to 0)
```

Deployments create a new ReplicaSet for each unique Pod template. During a rolling update, the new ReplicaSet scales up while the old scales down. Old ReplicaSets are kept (scaled to 0) to enable rollback.

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `KUBECONFIG` | Path to kubeconfig file |
| `KUBE_NAMESPACE` | Default namespace |

sections.title: Rolling updates and rollout
sections.body: A rolling update replaces Pods incrementally. Trigger by changing the Pod template (image, env, resources). Use `kubectl rollout status deployment/<name>` to watch progress. The rollout succeeds when all new Pods are Ready and all old Pods are terminated. If a new Pod fails to become Ready, the rollout stalls — use `kubectl rollout undo` to rollback. Set `maxSurge` and `maxUnavailable` to control speed vs availability tradeoff. Use `minReadySeconds` to add a delay before marking new Pods as available.

sections.title: Rollback
sections.body: Kubernetes stores rollout history as ReplicaSet revisions. Use `kubectl rollout history deployment/<name>` to list revisions. Use `kubectl rollout undo deployment/<name>` to rollback to the previous revision, or `--to-revision=N` for a specific one. Rollbacks are instant because the old ReplicaSet already exists — Kubernetes just scales it up and scales the current one down. Set `revisionHistoryLimit` (default 10) to control how many old ReplicaSets are retained.

sections.title: Scaling replicas
sections.body: Scale manually with `kubectl scale deployment/<name> --replicas=N` or by editing the manifest. For automatic scaling, use [[kubernetes/hpa]] to scale based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics. The scheduler distributes Pods across nodes for availability. Use `topologySpreadConstraints` or pod anti-affinity to ensure Pods spread across zones and nodes.

sections.title: Update strategies
sections.body: Two built-in strategies: **RollingUpdate** (default) — gradual replacement with maxSurge and maxUnavailable controls. **Recreate** — kill all old Pods first, then create new ones. Use Recreate when the app cannot tolerate two versions running simultaneously (database migrations, protocol changes). For canary deployments, use `kubectl rollout pause/resume` or dedicated tools like Argo Rollouts or Flagger.

scripts.name: Deploy Application
scripts.description: Create or update a Deployment and wait for rollout to complete

scripts.name: Check Rollout Status
scripts.description: Show deployment status, replica counts, and rollout history
scripts.inline:
#!/bin/bash
set -euo pipefail
NAME=${1:?Usage: rollout-status.sh <deployment-name>}
NS=${KUBE_NAMESPACE:-default}

echo "=== Deployment Status ==="
kubectl get deployment "$NAME" -n "$NS" -o wide
echo ""
echo "=== Rollout Status ==="
kubectl rollout status deployment/"$NAME" -n "$NS" 2>&1 || true
echo ""
echo "=== Rollout History ==="
kubectl rollout history deployment/"$NAME" -n "$NS"
echo ""
echo "=== ReplicaSets ==="
kubectl get replicasets -n "$NS" -l app="$NAME" -o wide

depends.reason: Deployments create and manage Pods through ReplicaSets
